data.title: Piper Sandler downgrades Globant after Q1 earnings miss; stocks tumble
data.description:
Globant (NYSE:GLOB) shares tumbled 30.2% in premarket trade after the firm missed its top and bottom line estimates and Piper Sandler lowered the stock to Neutral.

“While we had anticipated macro headwinds impacting revenue growth and had cut our estimates a month ago, we did not lower them enough,” analysts at Piper Sandler said.

The analyst added that while it believes that the firm is well-placed in the longer term given its offering, it will be difficult for the firm to take unique steps to drive meaningful upside to broader demand trends

“The company attributed its poor performance to the macro, with particular pressure in LATAM, at some of its largest customers and tariff-impacted industries,” Piper Sandler said in a note.

On Thursday, GLOB reported Q1 revenue of $611.1M and adjusted earnings per share of $1.50.

"We will continue our checks to look for an inflection point in the demand environment," the analysts noted. 

SA Quant rates the stock at Strong Sell, while SA analysts rate it at Hold.

data.title: Stock index futures rise as softer inflation data keep sentiments high
data.description:
Stock index futures were higher on Friday as the S&P 500 hoped to extend its four-day straight rally, while investors awaited the consumer sentiment report.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) +0.2%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) +0.2%, and Dow futures (INDU) +0.3%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 3 basis points to 4.40%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 3 basis points to 3.95%.

Wall Street on Thursday ended mixed, but its benchmark S&P 500 index extended a rally to four straight days and further solidified itself in positive territory for the year.

“Markets continued to advance over the last 24 hours, as weak U.S. inflation data and lower oil prices raised expectations that the Federal Reserve would still cut rates this year,” Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

There were several factors driving the rally, but a critically important one was the U.S. PPI data for April. It showed inflation pressures were much softer than expected, Allen added.

The April producer price index (PPI) report showed both headline and core producer prices unexpectedly declining on a M/M basis.

U.S. import and export prices surprised to the upside in April. 

Housing starts rose in April; building permits dropped more than expected.

On the economic side, the May consumer sentiment is expected to come in at 53.1.

data.title: Conduit Pharmaceuticals to execute 1-for-15 reverse stocksplit
data.description:
• Conduit Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:CDT) has approved a 1-for-15 reverse stock split of the company’s common stock.
  • The reverse stock split will become effective on May 19, 2025, and the common stock is expected to begin trading on a reverse stock split-adjusted basis on The Nasdaq Global Market on May 20, 2025.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Housing starts unexpectedly drop in April, building permits drop more than expected
data.description:
Housing starts ticked 1.6% M/M to a 1.3261M annual rate in April, compared to the 1.362M consensus and 1.334M in March (revised from 1.324M), according to data released by the U.S. Census Bureau on Thursday.

Still, privately-owned starts rose 1.6% to 1.361M from the March 2025 rate.

Single-family starts in April were at a rate of 927K, down 2.1% from the revised March mark of 947K. The April rate for units in buildings with five units or more stood at 420K.

Meanwhile, building permits fell 4.7% from a month earlier to 1.412M, compared with the 1.450M expected and 1.481M prior (revised).

Single family authorizations in April were at a rate of 922,000; this is 5.1 percent below the revised March figure of 972,000. 

Authorizations of units in buildings with five units or more were at a rate of 431,000 in April.
